title: Women at the Helm: Redefining India’s Development Story
content:
India's development narrative has decisively shifted from empowering women to being powered by women. From classrooms to cutting-edge research labs, women are claiming their space in STEM at an unprecedented pace, with 43% of higher education STEM enrolment and over 53% of UGC-NET research fellowships in 2024–25. Female enrolment has surged from 1.57 crore to 2.18 crore, while doctoral enrolment has grown by over 135% in under a decade. As India moves toward Viksit Bharat 2047, Nari Shakti is no longer a welfare goal but the engine of the nation’s economy.

India has taken significant strides toward women-led development. The Nari Shakti Vandan Adhiniyam (2023) ensures 33% reservation for women in Parliament and State Assemblies, while over 14.5 lakh women already serve in Panchayati Raj Institutions, strengthening grassroots democracy.

Economically, the Lakhpati Didi initiative is transforming rural livelihoods by integrating women-led Self-Help Groups into high-value sectors like drone technology, organic farming, and renewable energy. Over 2 crore women have already crossed the ₹1 lakh annual income mark, with a target of 3 crore Lakhpati Didis.

In science and innovation, programs like WISE-KIRAN and Vigyan Jyoti are bridging gender gaps. Women now constitute 18.6% of India’s R&D workforce, marking steady progress toward inclusive innovation leadership.

Financial inclusion has expanded through PM Jan Dhan Yojana and GeM Womaniya, where 56% of bank accounts belong to women, giving them direct financial control and boosting women-led MSMEs. At the same time, female labour force participation has reached 35.3% in December 2025, reflecting a structural shift driven by rural employment and entrepreneurship.

Women are also entering strategic domains like defence, with their numbers rising from 3,000 in 2014 to over 11,000 today, alongside growing participation in urban mobility systems such as Pink Buses and PM-eBus Sewa. Healthcare initiatives under Mission Shakti 2.0 further ensure life-cycle support through nutrition, maternal care, and financial security.

However, challenges remain significant. Women perform nearly 84% of unpaid care work, contributing an estimated 15–17% of GDP that remains unrecognized. Access to capital is limited, with women-led startups receiving only ₹4 out of every ₹100 raised. The leaky pipeline, gender pay gaps, and low leadership representation continue to hinder progress, with India ranking 131st in the Global Gender Gap Index 2025.

Social barriers like the Sarpanch-Pati phenomenon, climate vulnerability placing a triple burden on rural women, and occupational segregation—where 94% of women remain in the informal sector—further restrict advancement. Additionally, the rise of digital harassment, deepfakes, and AI bias creates new challenges for women’s participation in the digital economy.

Way forward: India must adopt gender-responsive urban planning, scale SHE-Mart cooperatives, and introduce returnship programs to support women re-entering the workforce. Investments in climate-resilient agri-tech, care economy infrastructure, and digital safety frameworks are essential.

Women-led development is now central to India’s growth story. By addressing structural barriers and strengthening institutional support, India can unlock the full potential of its women workforce and accelerate progress toward Viksit Bharat 2047 and Sustainable Development Goal 5.

title: Strengthening Democratic Institutions
content: Strong institutions are essential for transparency, accountability and governance.
